what: OZSpline stateful-harness feasibility probe (toward verifying the full Catmull-Rom evaluateCurve vs OZBezierInterpolator::interpolate).
proven_working: OZSplineState::OZSplineState() [C1] constructs into a 512-byte buffer (real size ~48B) without crash.
OZSpline::OZSpline(OZSplineState*) [C1] constructs into a 512-byte buffer (real size ~200B, internally new's an OZInterpolators) without crash.
OZSpline::addVertex(CMTime const&, double value, CMTime const& tangent, bool) adds keyframes without crash. CMTime = {int64 value; int32 timescale; uint32 flags=1; int64 epoch} (24 bytes).
remaining_to_call_interpolate: OZBezierInterpolator::interpolate(OZSpline&, CMTime const& t, void* outA, void* outB, CMTime const& , bool, bool) — the disasm calls getControlPoints internally then CMTime math, writing to the void* outputs (args x5/x6). Need to: (1) set the per-vertex INTERPOLATION TYPE to Catmull-Rom (type 6) — addVertex used a default; find the OZSpline setInterpolation / per-vertex interp setter (OZChannelImpl::setInterpolation(uint) exists); (2) map the two void* output pointers (likely value + derivative or control-point struct) by reading where interpolate stores its result; (3) confirm the ABI by reproducing a KNOWN 2-keyframe answer (e.g. linear 0->100 at t=0.5 => 50) BEFORE trusting any verdict.
go_no_go: GO on construction (proven). NO-GO on shipping until interpolate reproduces a known-answer case (S1-style harness gate). This is the next focused session's task; do NOT ship a spline harness whose interpolate output isn't first validated on a known value.
engine_side: engine/src/evaluator/curves.ts evaluateCurve (Catmull-Rom branch, interp 6) is the port to compare; it's already measured to 0.26px vs ruler-decode, so this harness would tighten that to exact.

interpolator_dispatch.discovery: interp type is NOT a simple field on the spline — it's dispatched via OZInterpolatorStrategies::getInterpolator(uint type) -> returns an OZBezierInterpolator OR OZSplineInterpolator (BOTH have interpolate(OZSpline&, CMTime, void*, void*, CMTime, bool, bool)). The engine's Catmull-Rom (curves.ts interp 6) maps to one of these; the per-vertex interp type is stored in the spline's vertex state.
interpolator_dispatch.next_session_path: (1) determine which interpolator + type-uint corresponds to Catmull-Rom (type 6) — read OZInterpolatorStrategies::getInterpolator dispatch or set via OZChannelImpl::setInterpolation(6) on a channel wrapping the spline; (2) set the vertex interp type before interpolate; (3) call interpolate with the two void* outputs allocated (double[] for value + aux); (4) GATE on a known 2-keyframe linear answer (0->100 @ t=0.5 => 50) before recording any verdict.
interpolator_dispatch.callable_confirmed: OZSplineState/OZSpline/addVertex construct + run without crash (proven). OZInterpolatorStrategies::getInstance/getInterpolator are exported + callable.

interpolate_call_attempt.result: Naive interpolate() call SEGFAULTS (exit 139), as gated-for. OZInterpolatorStrategies::getInstance()+getInterpolator(type) return valid interpolator pointers for types 0/1/6/15, but calling OZBezierInterpolator::interpolate(interp, spline&, t, out1, out2, z, false, false) crashes.
interpolate_call_attempt.why: interpolate calls getControlPoints internally which reads per-vertex INTERP-TYPE + tangent state that addVertex(default) did not populate, and/or the out-pointer/arg ABI needs the exact struct layout (the disasm shows it writes CMTime+double structs to x5/x6, not plain double[]). The spline vertices likely need their interp type set (via the channel wrapper OZChannelImpl::setInterpolation, or addVertex with tangent params) before interpolate is valid.
interpolate_call_attempt.disciplined_outcome: CORRECTLY did not ship — the known-answer gate (linear 0->100@0.5=50) was never reached because the call crashes. Construction (State/Spline/addVertex) is proven; interpolate needs the vertex interp-type + out-struct ABI decoded. This is a focused next-session RE task, precisely mapped. No shipped engine/harness code touched (all scratch in /tmp).
